title: Regular Expressions
subtitle: The re module is your Pattern/Matcher

sections.java: // Pattern + Matcher import java.util.regex.*; Pattern p = Pattern.compile("\\d{3}-\\d{4}"); Matcher m = p.matcher(text); if (m.find()) { String hit = m.group(); }
sections.python: # re.search scans anywhere in the string import re pattern = re.compile(r"\d{3}-\d{4}") # raw string! m = pattern.search(text) if m: hit = m.group()

sections.title: Always Use Raw Strings — and search() vs match()
sections.content: Write patterns as raw strings: r"\d+". Without the r, Python's own string layer eats the backslashes (you'd need "\\d+" like Java). Second trap: re.match() anchors at the start of the string (it does not mean Java's matches()), while re.search() scans anywhere — the analog of Matcher.find(). For a full-string match use re.fullmatch().

sections.java: // Groups, replace-all Pattern p = Pattern.compile("(\\w+)@(\\w+)"); Matcher m = p.matcher(s); if (m.find()) m.group(1); // user String out = s.replaceAll("\\s+", " ");
sections.python: # Named groups + re.sub m = re.search(r"(?P<user>\w+)@(?P<host>\w+)", s) if m: m.group("user") # by name m.groupdict() # {'user':..,'host':..} out = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", s) # replaceAll

sections.title: findall, finditer, and Named Groups
sections.content: re.findall(pattern, text) returns every match as a list in one call — no while (m.find()) loop. re.finditer yields match objects lazily for richer access. Named groups (?P<name>...) plus m.groupdict() turn a regex straight into a structured dict — perfect for parsing log lines or IDs in a data pipeline. Compile once with re.compile if you reuse the pattern in a loop.

sections.question: What does re.match(r"\d+", text) do that surprises Java developers?
options: It matches the whole string like Java's matches()
It only anchors at the START of the string — use re.search() to match anywhere
It returns a boolean, not a match object
It requires the pattern to be compiled first
sections.explanation: Despite the name, re.match() is not Java's matches() — it anchors only at the start of the string (closer to Matcher.lookingAt()). To find a match anywhere use re.search() (≈ find()); to require the entire string match use re.fullmatch().
